prompt:
Ticket: home-grown templating engine for the notification service.

We render every email/webhook body by string-concatenation right now and marketing keeps asking for things we can't do. I want a small but real templating engine, TypeScript, zero npm deps, that the rest of the service imports from ONE module: template.ts exporting `compile(src)` (returns a reusable `(data, opts?) => string`), `render(src, data, opts?)` and a `TemplateError` class. Behind that entry point, structure it properly — tokenizer/parser/AST, renderer, filter registry, escaping — as separate modules (3–8 files total), not one 500-line blob; I will reject a single-file PR.

What the language needs to do: `{{ path.to.value }}` interpolation with dot paths, HTML-escaped by default (& < > " '); `{{{ raw }}}` skips escaping. Filters with pipes, chained left to right: `{{ name | trim | upper }}` — builtins upper/lower/trim/json plus `default:"fallback"` which takes a quoted argument and kicks in for missing/empty values; callers can pass custom filters in opts (theirs win on a name clash); an unknown filter is a render-time TemplateError naming it. Blocks: `{{#if path}}...{{else}}...{{/if}}` (falsy = false, 0, empty string, null/undefined/missing, empty array) and `{{#each list}}...{{else}}...{{/each}}` exposing `{{this}}` and `{{@index}}`, item properties shadowing outer scope, blocks nesting arbitrarily; `{{#each}}` over a present non-array must throw. Missing-key policy comes from opts.missing: "empty" (default), "error" (TemplateError naming the path — judged AFTER filters run, so `default` can rescue it), or "keep" (re-emit the tag as `{{path}}`). Malformed templates — unclosed tags or blocks, mismatched or stray closers, floating {{else}}, empty {{}}, unknown block helpers — must all be TemplateError at compile time, not garbage output at render time.

The acceptance suite is test_template.ts, run with `node --test test_template.ts` — it imports only ./template.ts and it is the contract, down to exact output strings; read it before you design. Don't touch the test file.
